title: Envisioning better benchmarks for machine learning PDE solvers

data_scope.article_type: News & Views / Brief Communication (perspective, no new empirical data)
data_scope.primary_literature_basis: McGreivy & Hakim (2024), Nat. Mach. Intell. 6, 1256-1269 — systematic review of ML-PDE solver papers

core_conclusion: ML-based PDE solvers have been systematically compared against weak numerical baselines and with a reporting bias favouring positive results, creating an overly optimistic view of the field. The solution is to define open, rigorous benchmark challenges — analogous to ImageNet for computer vision or CASP for protein folding — that honestly expose where machine learning uniquely adds value over classical numerical methods.
why_it_matters: Diagnoses a structural credibility gap in scientific ML evaluations; frames benchmark absence as inherent to PDE diversity; calls for community-level competition infrastructure; identifies the specific criteria (speed, generalization, complex geometry) where ML may genuinely win.
why_nature: Field-level News & Views shaping evaluation culture in machine intelligence; responds to a companion high-profile study in the same journal; author is a leading neural-PDE researcher at JKU Linz; draws science-policy lessons applicable across ML subfields.
added_value_to_my_research: CASP-analogy benchmark design for GeoAI spatiotemporal prediction tasks with held-out spatial contexts
Formal spatial-leakage severity score inspired by the paper's weak-baseline critique for spatial ML evaluation
Transfer operator learning bridging PDE fragmentation -> transferable spatiotemporal graph operator for city-scale predictions
ML-weather surrogate models validated on rigorous multi-criteria benchmarks -> climate-driven urban health burden modelling
Urban-scale ML-CFD benchmark -> surrogate models for urban microclimate in healthy-city planning
